title: Smoking Sections
excerpt: I think the smoking sections at airports should be more thoughtful to the traveler.
body:
I think the smoking sections at airports should be more thoughtful to the traveler. Considering the desire associated with travel, the pressure associated with business, and the general risk involved with flying these days, smoking a cigarette before and after a flight should be a signature experience.

There are a few circumstances where a cigarette does not count:

- Pre-and-post flight
- Europe
- As an appetizer before your second martini
- After finishing the last page of a book
- on Christmas Post- dinner, pre-dessert with my grandmother (that one is personal to me)
- Inside if a restaurant, bar, or club allows

There are a few smoking sections that are civilized.

Upon landing in Seoul, the IN-CHEN International Airport has transparent rectangular pods directly outside of the terminals with ashtrays and automatic doors. This is good. You arrive so disoriented from America that the pods feel like some sort of waiting room. A smoked drenched limbo where you can prepare your mind for what's to come.

Two other smoking sections that come to mind are at Bob Hope Airport in Burbank and AHH--MER-E-GO Vespucci Airport in Florence, Italy. Meditative with no frills.

In Burbank, the smoking section is an all but forgotten alleyway where they shove smokers aside. It feels private and most importantly, provides shade from the Los Angeles sun. One bench, two ashtrays, and a few plants hanging on. In Italy at the Vespucci Airport, it is wherever you would like, with trash can sized ashtrays at your disposal. You realize you've landed in Italy, and there's many more cigarettes that lie ahead.

More important than smoking, while writing this at Jetblue Terminal 5 at LAX—whose smoking section is utterly deplorable, resembling more of a bus stop with no bench, I realized that there is much to see and many more airports to feel unhinged in. You must go through the discomfort caused in public spaces to recognize the type of person you want to be and who you want to surround yourself with. Once inside the Airport, I advise to relinquish control. It is the time for observation. Take notice of those you happen to share this world with.

The good, the bad, and the ugly.

Of course there are instances of mundanity and delays, and in these moments it would be nice to think fondly of the cigarette I just had, and the future cigarette I will inhale upon landing. A carefully considered smoking section is what ties it all together.

Positively harmful?

Absolutely. But when the time is right, smoking a cigarette is a wonderful way to keep you present. I'm not in any way insinuating that it's as good for you as let's say, yoga… but we all know life has always been about breathing in… and out.
